texto: Writing "Snugs the Snow Bear" was an exhilerating walk on a beach - I stopped now and then to study the rock pools, put my hand to my forehead, to gaze over the vast ocean, and the tide danced; played music at my feet, turned over precious shells and stones, returned them to me, polished. I picked one, held it to my ear. The tide rushed in, ran over my naked feet, and I was home.

texto: The song was a sea-shanty, and Snugs loved that kind of song. The music had an accordion, and the tune reminded him of the ship, and the songs Captain LightOwler played, when he was off duty, about pirates and the baddies on the sea.
explicacao: Citação de Suzy Davies sobre vínculos afetivos e cuidado mútuo. “The song was a sea-shanty, and Snugs loved that kind of song. The music had an accordion, and the…” articula familia, animals, bears em registro de familia, com relevância para contextos como bears e children-s-books.

texto: Books are a portable kind of time travel. We go back as well as forward when we read them. When we come back into the now, after being immersed in worlds previously unknown to us, we find ourselves, transformed. Touched by their magic, nothing we ever perceived beforehand remains quite the same.
explicacao: Citação de Suzy Davies sobre passagem do tempo e prioridades. “Books are a portable kind of time travel. We go back as well as forward when we read them. When w…” articula autoestima, books, magic em registro de autoestima, com relevância para contextos como mudanca e viagem.

texto: Writing which flows is more like play than work!
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Suzy Davies: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de temas ligados a trabalho. O trecho “Writing which flows is more like play than work!” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que trabalho, writers, writing pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de trabalho e writing.

frase_original: Writing which flows is more like play than work!

texto: Writing is like scrambling up a hill on all fours; you arrive halfway up, and you are committed - you want to reach the summit, yet you must stop, to gather your strength and will. When you have climbed to the summit, you stop, catch your breath, and take in the vista. You wonder how you made it. Then, you make your decent, somewhat subdued, all the while planning your next ascent, your next attempt to touch the sky. The memory of your climb leaves you with one impression - the idea that it gets easier, over time.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Suzy Davies: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de passagem do tempo e prioridades. O trecho “Writing is like scrambling up a hill on all fours; you arrive halfway up, and you are committed -…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que reflexao, writers, writing pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de writing e writing-life.

frase_original: Writing is like scrambling up a hill on all fours; you arrive halfway up, and you are committed - you want to reach the summit, yet you must stop, to gather your strength and will. When you have climbed to the summit, you stop, catch your breath, and take in the vista. You wonder how you made it. Then, you make your decent, somewhat subdued, all the while planning your next ascent, your next attempt to touch the sky. The memory of your climb leaves you with one impression - the idea that it gets easier, over time.
